title: Hamlet
summary: Shakespeare's tragedy of a grieving prince (written around 1600), commanded by his father's ghost to avenge a murder, who instead questions, doubts, and delays — the longest and most famous of his plays, and the work most often credited with putting a fully modern inner life on the stage. In his soliloquies, above all 'To be, or not to be,' a character first seems to think aloud and to possess a hidden self. Why Hamlet delays has never been settled, and the play survives in several differing early texts, with no single original.
hook: A prince comes home to a funeral and finds a wedding. His father the king is dead, his uncle wears the crown, and his mother has married that uncle. Then his father's ghost appears and says he was murdered, and demands revenge. In any older revenge story the son now sharpens his sword and strikes. Hamlet does not. He doubts the ghost, tests it, stages a play to catch his uncle, mocks himself for failing to act, and turns over the question of death itself. The whole tragedy is built around a man who will not do the one thing the plot demands. Out of that hesitation came a character who seems, for the first time in literature, to have a full inner life, and the most discussed play ever written.
aliases: The Tragedy of Hamlet, Prince of Denmark
indexTerms: To be or not to be

beginner.theWork:
A young man stands on the cold walls of a castle at midnight. He is waiting for a ghost. The ghost is his dead father, the old king of Denmark. When it appears, it tells him a terrible thing. The king did not die by accident. His own brother poisoned him, took the crown, and married the dead king's widow. The ghost wants Hamlet to make it right. It wants revenge.

This is the start of Hamlet. It was written by William Shakespeare, the most famous writer in the English language, around the year 1600. It is the longest play he ever wrote and the most famous play in the world.

Hamlet is a prince, and he is brilliant. He is also crushed by grief. His father is dead, his mother has remarried far too fast, and now a ghost is asking him to kill a king. A simpler hero would just act. Hamlet cannot. He thinks instead. He doubts. He waits. And that waiting is the whole strange heart of the play.
beginner.momentOfMaking:
Shakespeare did not invent this story from nothing. Tales of a prince who pretends to be mad while plotting revenge were already old. There was an ancient Danish legend, and there seems to have been an earlier play about Hamlet on the London stage before his. Shakespeare took a familiar revenge story and changed what it was about.

Revenge plays were popular in his time. Audiences loved blood, ghosts, and madness on stage. In a normal revenge play, the hero is held back by outside problems. He cannot reach the villain, or he must wait for the right moment. The drama comes from the obstacles.

Shakespeare did something different. He moved the obstacle inside the hero's own mind. Hamlet could kill his uncle. There is a scene where he stands right behind him with a sword. He does not strike. The thing stopping Hamlet is not a locked door. It is Hamlet himself. That single shift is what makes the play feel new even now.
beginner.whatItDoes:
The most powerful parts of Hamlet are the moments when he is alone on stage and speaks his thoughts aloud. These speeches are called soliloquies. In them, Hamlet seems to think in front of us. We hear a real mind working through grief, anger, fear, and doubt. No earlier character had ever seemed so fully alive on the inside.

The most famous of these begins with the words 'To be, or not to be.' In it, Hamlet wonders whether it is better to live or to die. He is thinking about suffering, about death, and about why people go on living when life is so painful. These are among the most quoted lines in all of English.

The play circles many huge subjects. It is about grief and a son who cannot let go of his father. It is about revenge and what it costs. It is about madness, both real and faked, since Hamlet acts mad and a young woman named Ophelia truly loses her mind. And it is about death. In a famous scene, Hamlet holds the skull of a dead jester named Yorick and thinks about how everyone, great or small, ends as bones.
beginner.whatItChanged:
Hamlet changed what people expected a character could be. Before it, characters in plays mostly showed what they felt through action. Hamlet shows us his mind. He is full of doubt and self-blame. He argues with himself. He is never quite sure of anything. That hidden, restless inner life is something we now expect from every serious novel and film.

Because of this, many people say Hamlet helped invent the modern idea of the self. The self as a private inner world, full of thoughts no one else can see, owes something to this play.

The play also gave English a huge store of phrases. People say 'something is rotten in the state of Denmark' when they sense hidden wrong. They speak of holding 'a mirror up to nature.' And the name Hamlet itself has become a kind of shorthand. When someone thinks and worries so much that they cannot act, we call them a Hamlet.
beginner.howBeenRead:
One question has followed Hamlet for centuries. Why does he delay? The ghost tells him what to do early on. Yet Hamlet waits almost the whole play before acting. People have never agreed on why, and that disagreement is part of why the play stays alive.

Some readers say Hamlet is simply too thoughtful. He thinks so much that he cannot bring himself to act. Others say he is right to be careful, because the ghost might be a lie or a trap, so he tests it first. Some say he is sunk in deep grief and despair, too sad to do anything. Others have wondered if the real problem is that the play itself never fully explains him.

One famous answer came from Sigmund Freud, the founder of modern psychology. He suggested that Hamlet secretly cannot punish his uncle because his uncle did what Hamlet himself, deep down, wished to do. This is a striking idea. But it is a guess about the character, not a fact in the play. Each of these readings is just that, a reading. The play hands you the question and leaves the answer open. That is exactly why people keep coming back to it.

general.theWork:
A prince stands at midnight on the walls of a castle and meets the ghost of his murdered father, who names the killer and demands revenge. Almost any earlier hero would act. Hamlet instead spends the play thinking, doubting, testing, and turning the question over, and that refusal to strike is the engine of the whole tragedy. Hamlet sits at an odd point in literary history. It is an old-fashioned revenge play on its surface, yet it has gone on feeling like the first genuinely modern portrait of a mind.

The outline is grim and simple. The king of Denmark is dead. His brother Claudius has taken the throne and married the dead king's widow, Gertrude, who is Hamlet's mother. The ghost tells Hamlet that Claudius poured poison in his ear, and asks for vengeance. Hamlet feigns madness, stages a play to expose Claudius, kills the wrong man by mistake, drives the young Ophelia to madness and death, and only at the very end, as the stage fills with the dead, takes his revenge. Around this plot Shakespeare built the longest of his plays, written about 1600, and the one most often called the greatest in the language.
general.momentOfMaking:
Shakespeare inherited the Hamlet story already shaped by long use. A version appears in a medieval Danish chronicle, in which a prince feigns idiocy to survive and avenge his father. The tale passed into a French collection of stories, and there seems to have been an earlier Hamlet play on the London stage in the 1580s, now lost, that Shakespeare's audience would have known. The bones of the plot, the murdered father, the usurping uncle, the feigned madness, the eventual revenge, were not his invention.

The revenge tragedy was a thriving form when Shakespeare wrote. These plays gave audiences ghosts crying for blood, delays and disguises, and a final stage strewn with bodies. In the standard pattern, the avenger is blocked by external obstacles, and the suspense comes from how he overcomes them. Shakespeare kept the machinery but relocated the obstacle. His Hamlet has the means and the opportunity to kill Claudius, and at one point stands over him with a drawn sword. What stops him is internal: doubt, conscience, calculation, or something the play never names. By moving the barrier inside the hero, Shakespeare turned a popular genre into a study of a single restless mind.
general.whatItDoes:
The play's deepest achievement is its inwardness, and it is carried above all by the soliloquies. Alone on stage, Hamlet thinks aloud, and the speeches do not merely report his feelings but seem to enact a mind in the act of reasoning, doubting, and recoiling. 'To be, or not to be,' the most famous lines in English, are not a decision but a meditation on whether the pain of living is worth bearing and why the fear of death holds people back. Through these speeches Hamlet became the first character in literature who seems to possess a hidden interior that exceeds anything he does.

Around that inwardness the play opens onto subject after subject without exhausting any of them. It is about grief, and a son who cannot mourn quietly. It is about revenge and the moral wreckage it leaves. It is about madness, real and feigned, since Hamlet performs lunacy while Ophelia genuinely loses her reason and drowns. It is about corruption, caught in the line that something is rotten in the state of Denmark. And it is about mortality, most memorably in the graveyard, where Hamlet lifts the skull of the jester Yorick and confronts the plain fact that all human distinction ends in bone. The play is famous for being inexhaustible, and this crowding of great themes is why.
general.whatItChanged:
Hamlet's most lasting effect was on what a literary character could be. Earlier dramatic figures revealed themselves chiefly through action and declaration. Hamlet reveals a self that is divided, self-questioning, and never fully transparent even to himself. He blames himself for inaction, mocks his own speeches, and circles his decisions without reaching them. That depiction of a hidden inner life, at odds with the world and with itself, became a model that later fiction and drama drew on constantly. The claim, associated with the critic Harold Bloom, that Shakespeare essentially invented the modern self rests heavily on this play.

The influence ran in two directions. It shaped the idea of inwardness, the sense that a person is most truly themselves in the private theater of their thoughts, which the novel would later make its central territory. And it seeded the language with phrases now worn smooth by use: 'to thine own self be true,' 'the lady doth protest too much,' 'the play's the thing.' Above all, the name became a concept. To call someone a Hamlet is to name the thinker who cannot act, the person paralyzed at the moment that demands a deed. Few literary characters have so fully detached from their text to become a common figure of speech.
general.howBeenRead:
The reading of Hamlet has organized itself for two centuries around a single question: why does the prince delay? The ghost issues its command early, and Hamlet has the chance to obey, yet he postpones his revenge almost to the end. The play offers no settled explanation, and the long argument over the gap is part of what has kept it alive.

The nineteenth century tended to locate the delay in Hamlet's character. The Romantics made him a man of such refined thought and sensibility that decisive action became impossible for him, an intellect too fine for the bloody task it was handed. Later critics, A.C. Bradley among the most influential, treated the question as a problem in Hamlet's psychology to be solved through close attention to the text. Others have read the hesitation as prudence rather than weakness, since the ghost may be a deceiving spirit and Hamlet is right to test it before killing a king. The poet T.S. Eliot took a sharply contrary line, arguing that the play fails to give Hamlet's emotion an adequate object, that his feeling exceeds anything the plot supplies, and that the work is therefore an artistic puzzle rather than a triumph.

The most famous modern reading came from outside literary study. Sigmund Freud, and his follower Ernest Jones, proposed that Hamlet cannot kill Claudius because Claudius has merely acted out Hamlet's own buried desire to remove his father and possess his mother. This is among the best-known interpretations of any literary work, but it remains an interpretation imposed on the play, not a fact the text states. Each answer, the Romantic, the psychological, the strategic, the Freudian, says as much about its age as about the prince, which is precisely why the question has never closed.

advanced.theWork:
Criticism of Hamlet has long been disciplined by a fact the popular reception tends to forget: there is no single text of the play. Hamlet survives in three substantively different early versions, the First Quarto of 1603, the Second Quarto of 1604–05, and the Folio text of 1623, and they diverge in ways that bear directly on interpretation. The First Quarto is roughly half the length of the others, reorders scenes, and gives a markedly different, more obviously deranged Hamlet; the Second Quarto and the Folio differ from each other by several hundred lines, including whole passages present in one and absent from the other. The conflated text most readers encounter, assembled by editors from the longer two, is an editorial artifact that corresponds to no version Shakespeare's contemporaries actually saw.

This is not a marginal technicality. The 'two-text' and 'three-text' debate, sharpened by the revisionist editing of the late twentieth century, reframes questions that the character-centered tradition had treated as questions about Hamlet's mind as instead questions about which Hamlet. The delay, the structure of the soliloquies, even the prince's apparent coherence as a psychological subject vary across the witnesses. The most disciplined current scholarship treats the multiplicity of texts not as noise obscuring a lost original but as the actual condition of the work, and reads the editorial drive toward a single authoritative Hamlet as itself a historical phenomenon requiring explanation.
advanced.momentOfMaking:
The material history of the play resists the romance of a single inspired composition. Beyond the textual plurality, there is the problem of the lost prior play, the so-called Ur-Hamlet, attested in the 1580s and sometimes linked to Thomas Kyd, whose Spanish Tragedy established the conventions of the revenge form Shakespeare was working within and against. We cannot recover that play, and so we cannot determine precisely how much of what feels distinctively Shakespearean, the interiorized delay above all, was his transformation of inherited material and how much was already present in the version his audience knew.

Stephen Greenblatt's argument that the play is shaped by the Reformation's destruction of Purgatory adds a further layer to the moment of making. The ghost claims to come from a place of purgatorial suffering, a Catholic category that English Protestant doctrine had abolished, so that the figure commanding the action occupies a theological status the play's own culture could no longer securely name. On this reading the uncertainty about the ghost, whether it is the father's spirit, a demon, or a delusion, is not merely a plot device enabling delay but a registration of a culture caught between two accounts of the dead. Whether this religious frame is the play's organizing concern or one resonance among many is exactly the kind of question the evidence underdetermines.
advanced.whatItDoes:
The soliloquies are the site where the play's claim to have invented literary interiority must be tested, and the test is harder than the popular formulation allows. The strong version of the claim, that Hamlet is the first character with a genuine inner life, cannot survive contact with the soliloquy traditions Shakespeare inherited, nor with the inwardness already available in earlier drama and devotional writing. The more defensible claim concerns degree and technique: the soliloquies stage thought as process rather than as report, allowing a character to surprise himself, to begin a speech not knowing where it will end, to revise and recoil in real time. Hamlet does not announce a settled interior; he appears to generate one as he speaks.

This produces the distinctive effect that the character seems to exceed the play, to possess a life and a withheld depth beyond what any line states. Margreta de Grazia's work pushes hard against the consensus this effect produced. She argues that the modern obsession with Hamlet's inwardness, the reading of him as a subject defined by hidden psychological depth, is a post-Romantic construction that detached the prince from the play's actual preoccupations, among them dispossession, inheritance, and the prince's loss of the land and succession that the older meaning of his situation foregrounds. The uncomfortable possibility her work raises is that the inwardness for which the play is most celebrated may be less in the text than in two centuries of reading trained to find it there.
advanced.whatItChanged:
The claim that Hamlet helped invent the modern self, most boldly stated by Harold Bloom, requires careful handling because it conflates several distinct propositions. One is genealogical: that later representations of interior consciousness, in the novel above all, descend from techniques Shakespeare developed here. Another is constitutive: that the modern experience of selfhood, the sense of an inner self hidden behind social performance, was in some measure shaped by this and similar texts. The first is a tractable question of literary influence; the second is a far larger and more contestable claim about the cultural formation of subjectivity, and it cannot be settled by literary analysis alone.

The genealogical claim is itself complicated by the recognition that influence runs partly backward, that we read Hamlet as the origin of modern interiority because later developments taught us to look for interiority and to find its source here. The play's status as the fountainhead of the modern self is to a significant degree an effect of its canonization, the same circular structure that attends Shakespeare's eminence generally: the work is read as foundational because the tradition that treats it as foundational selects and amplifies the features that support the reading. This does not make the claim false. It makes it a claim about a relationship between text and reception, rather than a property residing in the text alone, and the distinction is where the serious argument now takes place.
advanced.howBeenRead:
The structured confrontation in Hamlet criticism is between the character-centered tradition that takes the prince's interiority as the play's center and the textual and historical scholarship that treats that interiority as a construction to be explained rather than a datum to be analyzed. The first tradition, running from the Romantics through Bradley and into the psychoanalytic readings of Freud and Jones, asks what is wrong with Hamlet, why he delays, what his inner conflict consists in, and treats the play as the dramatization of a mind. Its power is that it answers to the overwhelming experience of the play in performance, the sense of a real consciousness on stage. Its vulnerability is that it must treat as a solid psychological object a figure who exists only in language, and in several incompatible versions of that language.

The second tradition presses exactly that vulnerability. The two-text scholars point out that the delay and the inwardness vary across the witnesses, so that the psychological subject the first tradition analyzes is partly an editorial composite. De Grazia argues that the inwardness itself is a historical projection. Greenblatt relocates the play's energy from psychology to theology and politics. The genuinely hard question is whether these are rival accounts or different levels of description, whether explaining the construction of Hamlet's interiority dissolves the experience of that interiority or merely sets it in its proper frame. The text does not adjudicate, and its refusal to do so, sustained across multiple early versions and four centuries of reading, may be less a puzzle awaiting solution than the durable structure that has made the play inexhaustible.

foundation.theStory: Hamlet asks a question that sounds simple and turns out to be bottomless: why does a man who knows what he must do fail to do it? Shakespeare took a stock revenge plot, a murdered king, a usurping uncle, a son charged with vengeance, and moved the obstacle from the world into the hero's mind. The result is a play whose center is not an action but a hesitation, and a character who seems, in his soliloquies, to think in front of us. That apparent inner life is why the play is so often credited with putting a modern mind on stage, and why it has been claimed by every later age as a mirror of itself.
foundation.theDebate: The central debate is why Hamlet delays, and it has never been settled. The Romantics saw a thinker too refined to act; psychoanalytic readers, following Freud, saw a buried Oedipal guilt; others see strategic caution, since the ghost may be lying, or read the delay as a flaw in the play rather than the character. None of these is simply the truth; each is a reading the text permits. A second, harder debate is textual: Hamlet survives in three different early versions that disagree in important ways, so part of the puzzle is which Hamlet we are even interpreting.
foundation.whyItMatters: Hamlet unlocks Shakespeare, the revenge tragedy, and the long history of how literature represents the inner self. It gave the language a hoard of common phrases and gave the culture a name for the thinker who cannot act. Understanding it means understanding how a single play came to stand for the idea that a person has a hidden interior worth dramatizing, and how a work accumulates centuries of incompatible readings without being exhausted by any of them.
questions: Shakespeare moved the obstacle to revenge from the outside world into Hamlet's own mind. What changes about a revenge story when the only thing stopping the hero is himself?

The play has been read as a study of grief, of cowardice, of conscience, of buried desire, and as a flawed play that cannot explain its hero. Does this multiplicity mean the play has no determinate meaning, or that it is richer than any single answer can hold?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The scholarly conversation divides between a character-centered tradition and a textual-historical one. The first, running from the Romantics through A.C. Bradley and into the psychoanalytic reading of Freud and Ernest Jones, treats the play as the dramatization of a mind and asks why Hamlet delays. T.S. Eliot's dissent, that the play fails to supply an adequate object for Hamlet's emotion, belongs here as a sharp internal critique. The second tradition shifts the ground. The two-text and three-text scholars stress that Hamlet exists in several divergent early versions, so the psychological subject earlier critics analyzed is partly an editorial composite. Margreta de Grazia argues that the celebrated inwardness is a post-Romantic projection that detached the prince from the play's concerns with dispossession and inheritance. Stephen Greenblatt relocates the play's energy toward the Reformation's abolition of Purgatory and the resulting uncertainty about the ghost. Current work tends to hold the experience of Hamlet's interiority together with the awareness that this interiority has a history.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidential base is unusually fraught for a canonical work. There is no single authoritative text: the First Quarto of 1603, the Second Quarto of 1604–05, and the Folio of 1623 differ in length, ordering, and content, and the familiar text is an editorial conflation. There is a lost prior play, the Ur-Hamlet, attested in the 1580s, that we cannot read, so we cannot fully separate Shakespeare's innovations from his inheritance. Shakespeare left no statement of intention, no letters, no commentary on the play. The limitation this imposes is severe: the question of why Hamlet delays cannot be resolved by appeal to a stable text or a documented intention, because neither exists. The interpretive multiplicity is a genuine condition of the work, not a gap that better evidence would close.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong argument about Hamlet must decide how to treat the delay, and the most defensible move is to stop treating it as a problem with a hidden solution. Rather than supplying the missing motive the Romantics and the psychoanalysts each tried to supply, a serious reading can argue that the play withholds a determinate motive by design, and that the withholding is what produces the impression of a depth exceeding the text. Such an argument must engage the textual evidence honestly, conceding that the delay varies across the three early versions and that 'Hamlet's psychology' is partly an editorial and post-Romantic construction. It must also take a position on the inwardness claim: not whether Hamlet has an inner life, which is a category mistake about a figure made of language, but how the soliloquies produce the effect of one, and why later readers were trained to locate the origin of the modern self precisely here.
questions: Freud read Hamlet's delay as buried Oedipal guilt; de Grazia reads the focus on his inwardness as a later projection onto the play. What evidence would bear on the choice between these, and can the question be settled from the text at all?

Hamlet survives in three different early versions with no single original. How should the absence of a stable text change the way we argue about the character's psychology, and what would it mean to interpret 'the play' when there are several?

research.topic: Hamlet's debt to the revenge-tragedy tradition and its inherited sources
research.content: It is well established that Shakespeare did not invent the Hamlet story but inherited it. The core narrative appears in the medieval Danish account associated with Saxo Grammaticus, in which a prince feigns idiocy to survive and avenge his father, and passed into the European literary tradition through François de Belleforest's French Histoires tragiques. A lost earlier play on the Hamlet subject, the so-called Ur-Hamlet, is attested on the London stage in the late 1580s and is sometimes linked to Thomas Kyd, whose Spanish Tragedy fixed the conventions of the Elizabethan revenge play: the ghost crying for vengeance, the feigned madness, the play-within-a-play, the bloody catastrophe. Shakespeare's distinctive contribution, documented through comparison with these sources, is the interiorization of the avenger's delay, the relocation of the obstacle from external circumstance into the hero's own mind. The inheritance and the transformation are both secure conclusions of scholarship; what cannot be recovered is the precise content of the lost prior play.

research.topic: The relationship among the three early texts and its consequences for interpretation
research.content: Scholarship on Hamlet's textual situation has grown markedly more sophisticated as editorial theory has shifted from the goal of reconstructing a single lost original toward taking the surviving versions seriously as distinct texts. Hamlet exists in three substantively different early printings: the short First Quarto of 1603, the long Second Quarto of 1604–05, and the Folio text of 1623, the last two differing from each other by several hundred lines. The First Quarto's status, whether a memorial reconstruction, an early draft, or a version adapted for performance, remains debated. The emerging position treats the multiplicity not as corruption to be cleared away but as the actual condition of the work, with consequences for how the delay, the soliloquies, and the prince's coherence as a psychological figure are understood. How far the conflated reading text most readers know distorts interpretation, and whether the divergent Hamlets should be read as separate works, are active and unsettled questions.

research.topic: Why Hamlet delays, and what Shakespeare intended the delay to mean
research.content: What evidence would resolve this: a clear statement by Shakespeare, in correspondence or commentary, of how he understood Hamlet's hesitation, together with a single stable text in which the delay takes a determinate form. Why unavailable: Shakespeare left no letters, no diary, and no critical reflection on his plays; the prefatory and choral apparatus that frames some works is absent here; and there is no single authoritative Hamlet but three divergent early versions in which the delay is structured differently, with a lost prior play behind them all that cannot be read. What can be inferred: that Shakespeare worked within an established revenge-tragedy convention and altered it by interiorizing the obstacle (established through source comparison), that the soliloquies stage thought as process in a way that produces the impression of a hidden depth, and that the play systematically withholds a single determinate motive. What cannot be established: whether the delay expresses a coherent psychology Shakespeare intended, a deliberate refusal to supply one, or an unevenness left by composition across an inherited and revised text, and therefore whether the Romantic, psychoanalytic, strategic, and skeptical readings recover an intention or project a later need onto the play.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Mind That Cannot Act
comparativeNarrative.content: For the Romantics and their heirs, Hamlet is above all a portrait of a mind. Coleridge described a man in whom thought had overwhelmed the power to act, and admitted he found a touch of Hamlet in himself. On this view the delay is the whole point. Hamlet is the thinker whose very depth disables him, too aware of every outcome to strike a single blow. The Victorian critic A.C. Bradley carried this into careful study, treating the prince as a real person whose inner conflict could be traced scene by scene. Freud and his follower Ernest Jones pushed the idea further, finding in the delay a buried wish that makes Claudius a mirror of Hamlet's own. Its strength is that it matches the experience of the play in performance, the sense of a real mind on stage. Its weakness is that it treats a figure made of words, in several different versions, as if he were a person with a hidden past to recover.

comparativeNarrative.name: Which Hamlet?
comparativeNarrative.content: For textual scholars, the most striking fact about Hamlet is one most readers never register. There is no single play to interpret. Three early versions survive, and they differ in ways that matter. The First Quarto of 1603 is about half the length of the others and gives a cruder, more openly mad prince. The Second Quarto and the Folio differ from each other by hundreds of lines. The Hamlet most people read is a modern blend of the two longer texts, a version no Elizabethan ever saw. This changes every other debate. When critics ask why Hamlet delays, the textual scholar asks first which Hamlet they mean, because the delay and the soliloquies are not the same across the versions. The tradition that reads the prince as a steady psychological figure is, from this angle, partly studying something editors assembled. The argument is not about Hamlet's character but about whether one character firm enough to carry all that study exists at all.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Ghost and the Abolished Afterlife
comparativeNarrative.content: A more recent line of reading sets the play back into the religious crisis of its moment. Stephen Greenblatt has stressed that the ghost claims to come from purgatorial suffering, the very idea the English Reformation had abolished a generation before. To a Protestant audience, Purgatory was a discredited Catholic teaching. So the figure who starts the whole plot holds a status the culture could no longer name with confidence: father's spirit, lying demon, or delusion. On this view, Hamlet's doubt about whether to trust the ghost is not only a personal failing or a way to stall. It marks a society caught between two accounts of the dead. The reading pulls the play's energy away from private psychology toward the public wound of religious upheaval. Its limit is also its insight. It lifts up one strand, the religious one, in a play that also presses hard on grief, desire, and inheritance, and the evidence does not prove it is the main frame rather than one among several.

comparativeSummary: These three views do not so much disagree about Hamlet as look at different things under the same name. The Romantic and psychological tradition reads the prince as a mind, and treats the delay as a symptom to be diagnosed. The textual scholars answer that there is no single prince to diagnose, because three early versions of the play disagree about the very speeches and choices the first tradition studies. The historical critics move the play's center from psychology to the religious crisis that made the ghost so uncertain in the first place. Held together, they show why the play has never run dry. The question it is famous for posing, why Hamlet delays, turns out to rest on earlier questions, which Hamlet and in which world, that the work itself refuses to close.

rabbitHole.label: Shakespeare
rabbitHole.reason: Hamlet is the supreme tragedy of the playwright who wrote it, and the work most often used to argue that Shakespeare invented the modern dramatic self. Understanding Shakespeare's commercial theater, his sources, and the survival of his plays in the 1623 Folio is essential to seeing how Hamlet came to be and how it came down to us.

rabbitHole.label: Greek Tragedy
rabbitHole.reason: Hamlet inherits and remakes the ancient tradition of the noble protagonist destroyed by a fatal entanglement. Greek tragedy fixed the form of the high-born figure brought down through action and fate; Hamlet keeps the dignity of the fall but moves the catastrophe inward, into a mind that hesitates rather than acts.

rabbitHole.label: Faust
rabbitHole.reason: Faust is the other towering European drama of a restless modern mind that has exhausted ordinary knowledge and reaches past every limit. Both works center a brilliant, dissatisfied protagonist whose inner striving drives the action, and both have been read by each later age as a mirror of the modern condition itself.

rabbitHole.label: The Divine Comedy
rabbitHole.reason: Dante's epic is the other supreme poem of a soul confronting death, judgment, and the question of what lies beyond. Hamlet's graveyard meditations and his uncertainty about the ghost's origin turn on the same matter of the dead and the afterlife that the Comedy maps in full, approached now as open question rather than fixed cosmology.

rabbitHole.label: Romanticism
rabbitHole.reason: Romanticism made the brooding, inward Hamlet its hero, recasting the prince's delay as the mark of a sensibility too fine for action. The reading of Hamlet as the thinker paralyzed by his own depth is largely a Romantic creation, and it shaped how the play was understood for the next two centuries.

rabbitHole.label: The Novel
rabbitHole.reason: The novel made the hidden inner life its central territory, the same interiority that Hamlet's soliloquies first staged on the public stage. The techniques for rendering a mind at odds with itself and with the world, which the play pioneered in dramatic speech, became the defining business of later prose fiction.

reference.author: Shakespeare, William (ed. Harold Jenkins)
reference.title: Hamlet (Arden Shakespeare, Second Series)
reference.annotation: The long-standard scholarly edition, with Jenkins's exhaustive treatment of the textual problem and the play's sources. The place to turn for a reader who wants the play together with a full account of why its text is unstable and where its story came from.

reference.author: Shakespeare, William (eds. Ann Thompson and Neil Taylor)
reference.title: Hamlet and Hamlet: The Texts of 1603 and 1623 (Arden Shakespeare, Third Series)
reference.annotation: The modern edition that prints the divergent early texts rather than conflating them, making the three-text problem visible on the page. Essential for understanding the editorial argument that there is no single Hamlet to interpret.

reference.author: Bradley, A.C.
reference.title: Shakespearean Tragedy
reference.annotation: The classic statement of the character-centered tradition, treating Hamlet as a coherent psychological being whose inner conflict can be reconstructed from the text. Foundational for the reading the entry sets against later textual and historical criticism, and still the clearest case for taking the prince's mind as the play's center.

reference.author: Greenblatt, Stephen
reference.title: Hamlet in Purgatory
reference.annotation: The influential study relocating the play's energy toward the Reformation's abolition of Purgatory and the resulting uncertainty about the ghost's status. Essential for the historical reading that shifts the question from Hamlet's psychology to the religious crisis of his moment.

reference.author: de Grazia, Margreta
reference.title: Hamlet without Hamlet
reference.annotation: The major modern challenge to the inwardness consensus, arguing that the obsession with Hamlet's interiority is a post-Romantic construction that detached the prince from the play's concerns with dispossession and inheritance. Foundational for the entry's account of how the modern Hamlet was made by his readers.

reference.author: Jones, Ernest
reference.title: Hamlet and Oedipus
reference.annotation: The full development of the Freudian reading, in which Hamlet cannot kill Claudius because Claudius has enacted his own buried desire. The entry treats it as the best-known instance of an interpretation imposed on the play rather than drawn from it, and the place to find that argument made at length.

reference.author: Eliot, T.S.
reference.title: Hamlet and His Problems (in The Sacred Wood)
reference.annotation: The famous dissent arguing that the play fails to supply an adequate object for Hamlet's emotion and is therefore an artistic problem rather than a triumph. The entry invokes it as the sharpest internal critique of the character-centered tradition.

reference.author: Saxo Grammaticus (trans. Oliver Elton)
reference.title: The Danish History (Gesta Danorum), Books I–IX
reference.annotation: The medieval Danish account containing the Amleth story of a prince who feigns idiocy to avenge his father, the ultimate source behind the play. The entry cites it as the origin of the inherited plot that Shakespeare transformed by interiorizing the delay.

commerce.title: Hamlet (Arden Shakespeare, Third Series)
commerce.author: William Shakespeare, eds. Ann Thompson and Neil Taylor
commerce.note: If you read only one thing, make it the play in this edition. The annotation gives clear help with the language and the famous textual puzzle, so a first-time reader is never lost and a returning reader sees why there is no single Hamlet.

commerce.title: Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human
commerce.author: Harold Bloom
commerce.note: Read this if you want the boldest case for why Hamlet matters. Bloom argues the play essentially invented the modern self; his chapters on Hamlet are exhilarating and overstated, which makes them the best provocation for a reader new to the debate.

commerce.title: Hamlet in Purgatory
commerce.author: Stephen Greenblatt
commerce.note: For the reader ready to see the play through its own moment rather than ours. Greenblatt shows how the Reformation's abolition of Purgatory unsettles the ghost, and turns the play's central uncertainty into a window onto a society arguing about the dead.

commerce.title: Hamlet without Hamlet
commerce.author: Margreta de Grazia
commerce.note: For readers who have absorbed the standard 'Hamlet's inner life' reading and want it dismantled. De Grazia argues that interiority is a later projection onto the play, recovering the questions of land and inheritance the modern obsession with the prince's mind buried.

commerce.title: Shakespearean Tragedy
commerce.author: A.C. Bradley
commerce.note: For the reader who wants the great classic statement of the case Bloom and the Romantics inherit. Bradley's close analysis of Hamlet's mind is the model the modern textual critics react against, and it remains the most thorough version of the character-centered reading.

commerce.title: Hamlet
commerce.author: William Shakespeare
commerce.note: The work itself, read as drama: the ghost on the walls, the feigned madness, the soliloquies, and the stage full of the dead at the end. The single best way to meet Hamlet is to read the play straight through, aloud where you can, before reading anyone's commentary on it.

popularCulture.title: Hamlet (Laurence Olivier, 1948)
popularCulture.description: Olivier's black-and-white film won Best Picture and fixed Hamlet in many minds as the brooding, melancholy prince. It opens by calling the play the story of a man who could not make up his mind, which neatly states the Romantic reading and quietly cuts the other answers. It is gorgeous, slow, and heavily trimmed, dropping whole characters to keep the focus on the prince.

popularCulture.title: To be, or not to be
popularCulture.description: These six words are the most quoted line in English, and most people who say them have never seen the play. They get used as a joke about any hard choice, far from Hamlet's actual subject, which is whether the pain of living is worth bearing. The phrase has floated free of the speech, a scrap of a play many admire but few have read.

popularCulture.title: Something is rotten in the state of Denmark
popularCulture.description: People say this when they sense hidden wrongdoing somewhere in a group or a government. In the play a guard says it as the ghost appears, hinting that the whole kingdom is sick at its core. Stripped of its scene, it has become plain everyday English for corruption you can smell but cannot yet prove.

popularCulture.title: The Lion King (1994)
popularCulture.description: Disney's animated film borrows Hamlet's bones: a king murdered by his jealous brother, a son who flees and must return to claim his throne, even a ghostly father in the clouds urging him on. It keeps the shape of the revenge story but throws out the doubt and delay that define Hamlet, giving children a hero who simply grows up and acts.

popularCulture.title: Rosencrantz and Guildenstern Are Dead (Tom Stoppard, 1966)
popularCulture.description: Stoppard retells Hamlet from the edge, following two minor courtiers who barely understand the deadly plot sweeping them along. The play is funny and bleak at once, and it captures how most people feel caught up in events bigger than they can grasp. It assumes you already know Hamlet, so it lands hardest for those who do.
